_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 1
choices: ValueError
TypeError
KeyError
FileNotFoundError
questions.explanation: The argument is a string, which is an accepted input type, but its contents do not represent a whole integer. That makes the value invalid.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 2
questions.prompt: Which block runs after try whether an exception occurred or not?
choices: finally
except
else
raise
questions.explanation: `finally` is reserved for work required on both paths. An except block needs a matching failure, while else requires success.

questions.title: Trace Exceptions
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
accepted: Credits must be a whole number
questions.explanation: Only conversion is protected, and only ValueError is handled. The response matches the failure the program understands. The resulting output is 'Credits must be a whole number'.

questions.title: Exceptions good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Exceptions?
choices: Protect the smallest operation that can fail.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Protect the smallest operation that can fail. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices: Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: A broad handler can make an unrelated NameError look like bad input. Catching ValueError keeps other bugs honest and visible.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Do not silence evidence'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
An empty `except` lets the program continue without completing its work or explaining why. Handle the expected case, log useful context, or allow the exception to reach a layer that can respond.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: An empty `except` lets the program continue without completing its work or explaining why. Handle the expected case, log useful context, or allow the exception to reach a layer that can respond.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Exceptions?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Catch the most specific useful exception.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Catch the most specific useful exception. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Exceptions?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Give the caller or user a recovery path.
questions.explanation: Give the caller or user a recovery path. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Exceptions?
choices: Keep unexpected programming errors visible.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Keep unexpected programming errors visible. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 10
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Exceptions?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Use `finally` only for cleanup shared by both outcomes.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use `finally` only for cleanup shared by both outcomes. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.
